metadata.role: Small supplemental mushroom species-content improvements for high-use records. Kept separate to avoid rewriting large canonical data chunks.
metadata.source_quality_note: Species statements are based on mycology references linked in each record. Complex records deliberately keep conservative wording.
metadata.notes: v4.3.61-r2026-05-14-mushroom-completion-wave1-15species: completed selected mushroom species records in-place with rights metadata and stale review flags cleared.

records.overview: A cool-weather oyster mushroom on hardwood that grows in overlapping shelf-like clusters. Use this record for the darker, colder-season oyster rather than warm-season phoenix oyster or aspen-focused oyster records.
records.field_identification: Look for overlapping shelf-like clusters on dead or living hardwood, whitish gills running down a short lateral stem or pseudostem, firm white flesh that does not change when cut, and a white to faintly lilac spore print. Season and host matter: this record fits the colder October-to-early-April oyster window.
records.culinary_uses: A good edible oyster mushroom when the whole cluster is young, fresh, and positively identified. Trim tough stem bases; older shelves become leathery and are better skipped.
records.edibility_notes: Do not treat every pale shelf mushroom as an oyster. Confirm growth on wood, decurrent pale gills, lack of ring/volva, white to lilac spore print, and the right season/host pattern.
records.general_notes: Cold season and non-aspen hardwood help separate this record from phoenix oyster and aspen oyster. Pale old specimens can overlap visually, so use several traits together.
records.mushroom_card_note: Cool-weather hardwood oyster; check decurrent pale gills, lateral stem, white/lilac spore print, and cold-season fruiting.

mushroom_profile.spore_print: White to faintly yellowish or lilac
mushroom_profile.season_note: October through early April in cool weather; separates it from warmer-season oyster species more reliably than cap color alone.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Pleurotus ostreatus
use_links.notes: Cold-weather oyster traits, hardwood habit, decurrent gills, and spore print.

records.overview: A warmer-season oyster mushroom on hardwood. Compared with pearl oyster, it is often paler, smaller, and more likely to show a short stem, but season and substrate are the more practical field separators.
records.field_identification: Look for pale whitish to beige fan- or lung-shaped caps in shelf-like clusters on hardwood, whitish decurrent gills, white flesh, short eccentric or lateral stems when present, and a whitish to grayish/lilac spore print. The late-April-through-September season is a major clue.
records.culinary_uses: A good edible oyster mushroom when young, fresh, and confidently identified. Use as a warm-season oyster record rather than forcing summer finds into the pearl-oyster entry.
records.edibility_notes: Confirm wood growth, decurrent pale gills, lack of ring/volva, and pale spore print. Do not rely only on cap color; faded pearl oysters and pale summer oysters can overlap.
records.general_notes: This record intentionally separates warm-season oysters from colder-season pearl oysters. Aspen-associated oysters may need the aspen-oyster record instead.
records.mushroom_card_note: Warm-season hardwood oyster; often paler and smaller than pearl oyster, with season doing much of the separating work.

stem_feature: Often more stemmed than pearl oyster
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late April through September; warm-weather oyster window.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Pleurotus pulmonarius
use_links.notes: Warm-season oyster traits and comparison with pearl oyster.

records.overview: A cold-weather wood mushroom with a sticky orange-brown cap, white spore print, and a tough stem that becomes dark and velvety from the base upward. It deserves a caution-heavy entry because deadly Galerina can also grow on wood.
records.field_identification: Look for clusters on hardwood stumps, logs, roots, or buried wood; sticky orange to reddish-brown caps; creamy-white to pale orange gills attached to the stem; no ring; white spore print; and a tough stem that darkens from the base upward into a brown-black velvety coating.
records.culinary_uses: Edible by tradition when correctly identified, but this guide treats it as a caution mushroom rather than a casual beginner target. Wild velvet foot is not shaped like grocery-store enoki.
records.edibility_notes: Do not collect unless the white spore print, lack of ring, dark velvety stem base, cold-season fruiting, and wood growth are all clear. Deadly Galerina has rusty-brown spores and may have a ring or ring zone.
records.general_notes: Wood can be buried, making clusters appear to fruit from soil. The dark velvety lower stem is one of the key field characters; a spore print is not optional on look-alike-prone brown/orange gilled mushrooms.
records.mushroom_card_note: Cold-weather wood mushroom; white spore print and dark velvety stem base are key. Compare carefully with deadly Galerina.
records.look_alike_notes: Deadly Galerina grows on wood and has rusty-brown spores, small brown caps, and often a thin ring or ring zone. Do not use cap color alone.

mushroom_profile.host_certainty: important but sometimes hidden by buried wood
mushroom_profile.season_note: Usually fall through spring; one of the few edible-type gilled mushrooms worth checking in cold weather.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Flammulina velutipes
use_links.notes: Cold-weather habitat, sticky cap, white spore print, and velvety stem traits.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Galerina marginata
use_links.notes: Deadly wood-inhabiting look-alike with rusty-brown spore print and possible ring zone.

records.scientific_name: Cantharellus spp.; conifer-associated yellow chanterelle complex, including C. enelensis-like material where appropriate
records.overview: A conservative field-guide record for yellow chanterelles associated with conifer woods. Treat this as a complex/group entry, not a promise that every find is one exact named species.
records.field_identification: Look for firm yellow to orange-yellow chanterelles growing from the ground in conifer or mixed-conifer woods, with blunt forked ridges or false gills running down the stem, solid flesh, no true blade-like gills, and often a fruity/apricot odor. Do not use this entry for orange true-gilled mushrooms growing in dense clusters from wood.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible group when positively identified. Clean grit from ridges and cook; avoid waterlogged, bug-riddled, or uncertain specimens.
records.edibility_notes: The key safety split is chanterelle false gills and ground/mycorrhizal habit versus jack-o'-lantern true gills and clustered wood/root growth. False chanterelle has soft, true forked gills and a flimsier build.
records.general_notes: North American golden/yellow chanterelles have been split into multiple species. Host association, region, and morphology help, but exact naming may require expert or molecular work. Species-complex wording is intentionally retained; exact species can require expert-level confirmation.
records.mushroom_card_note: Conifer-associated yellow chanterelle complex; use false gills, firm flesh, ground habit, and conifer association together.
records.look_alike_notes: Jack-o'-lantern has true orange gills and grows in clusters from wood or buried roots. False chanterelle has true forked orange gills and a softer, flimsier body than chanterelles.

mushroom_profile.spore_print: Yellowish to pale yellow/orange-yellow depending on species
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September; peak often mid-to-late summer depending on rainfall.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Jack-o'-lantern comparison
use_links.notes: True-gilled clustered wood/root look-alike for chanterelles.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: False chanterelle comparison
use_links.notes: Soft true-gilled chanterelle look-alike often near conifer litter or rotting wood.

use_links.label: Cantharellus enelensis background
use_links.notes: Background on a conifer-associated North American golden chanterelle species and the C. cibarius complex.

records.review_note: Species-complex wording should stay conservative; exact species may require expert or molecular confirmation.

records.scientific_name: Cantharellus spp.; hardwood-associated yellow chanterelle complex, including smooth/yellow chanterelle forms where appropriate
records.overview: A conservative field-guide record for yellow chanterelles associated with hardwoods, especially oak/hickory-type woods. Treat this as a complex/group entry rather than a single guaranteed species name.
records.field_identification: Look for firm yellow to orange-yellow chanterelles growing from soil in hardwood woods, with blunt wrinkles/ridges or false gills running down the stem, solid flesh, and a fruity/apricot odor. Smooth chanterelle forms may have a nearly smooth to shallowly wrinkled underside rather than obvious false gills.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible group when positively identified. Clean carefully and cook; discard old, slimy, heavily bug-riddled, or uncertain finds.
records.edibility_notes: Separate from jack-o'-lantern by ground/mycorrhizal habit, firm flesh, and blunt ridges/false gills rather than true blade-like orange gills. Separate from false chanterelle by sturdier flesh and non-true-gilled underside.
records.general_notes: Hardwood-associated yellow chanterelles in eastern North America include multiple closely related forms. Smooth chanterelle-type material can blur into more ridged yellow chanterelles, so the app keeps this record intentionally broad. Species-complex wording is intentionally retained; exact species can require expert-level confirmation.
records.mushroom_card_note: Hardwood-associated yellow chanterelle complex; oak/hickory association, firm flesh, and false-gill/smooth-ridge underside matter.
records.look_alike_notes: Jack-o'-lantern has true gills and clustered wood/root growth. False chanterelle has true forked orange gills and a softer, flimsier body.

staining: May bruise yellowish to orangish brown
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Pale pinkish-yellow to yellowish depending on species/form
mushroom_profile.gill_attachment: False gills/ridges or shallow wrinkles running down stem
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Cantharellus lateritius
use_links.notes: Smooth chanterelle traits, oak/hickory association, underside variation, and season.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Jack-o'-lantern comparison
use_links.notes: True-gilled clustered wood/root look-alike for chanterelles.

records.review_note: Species-complex wording should stay conservative; exact species may require expert or molecular confirmation.

records.overview: A soft orange true-gilled mushroom that commonly appears as a chanterelle look-alike. This guide keeps it as a caution record rather than a food record.
records.field_identification: False chanterelle has true, narrow, repeatedly forked orange gills that run down the stem; a soft, flimsy body compared with firm chanterelles; a finely velvety cap surface; white spore print; and a saprobic habit on forest litter, woody debris, or well-rotted wood, often under conifers.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat in this guide. It is included only to help separate true chanterelles from orange true-gilled look-alikes.
records.edibility_notes: Reports on edibility vary, but it is not a beginner edible and may cause digestive problems. Treat it as an avoid/caution mushroom in this app.
records.general_notes: The practical field split from chanterelles is underside texture and body feel: true chanterelles have blunt ridges or folds and firm flesh; false chanterelle has true forked gills and a softer build.
records.mushroom_card_note: Chanterelle look-alike with true forked orange gills and soft flesh; not treated as edible here.
records.look_alike_notes: Unlike chanterelles, this has true forked gills and a soft, flimsy body. Jack-o'-lantern also has true gills but tends to grow in clusters from wood or buried roots.

mushroom_profile.host_certainty: saprobic association, not mycorrhizal host proof
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; often under conifers, near woody debris, or from well-rotted wood.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Hygrophoropsis aurantiaca
use_links.notes: True forked gills, soft flesh, white spore print, and conifer/woody debris habitat.
